title: Share of deaths from suicide
subtitle: Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.
caption:
Share of deaths from suicide

Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

Across seven named countries from 1980 to 2023, the estimated share of deaths from suicide varies widely in both level and trajectory, with values ranging from under 0.5% to above 6%. The vertical axis measures the share of deaths from suicide as a percentage, with gridlines at 1% intervals from 0% to 6%. South Korea’s line begins near 2.3% in 1980, rises steadily through the 1990s and 2000s, peaks above 6% around 2010, then declines to approximately 4% by 2023. Japan’s line starts near 3.1%, climbs to about 3.6% in the mid-1980s, falls to roughly 2.5% by the mid-1990s, rises again to 3.3% around 2000, then trends downward to below 2% by 2023. The United States line remains between 1.5% and 1.8% for most of the period, ending near 1.7%. Germany’s line stays within the 1.2% to 1.6% band throughout, finishing near 1.3%. The United Kingdom line hovers around 1% for the entire span, ending slightly above 1%. Peru’s line begins near 0.2%, rises to about 0.7% by 2000, dips slightly, then climbs to nearly 0.8% by 2023. Greece’s line stays below 0.5% for the full period, ending near 0.4%. The most visible pattern is the pronounced rise and fall of South Korea’s share, which reaches the highest value on the chart and then declines substantially over the following decade.
